sanskrit_devanagari:
तत्र तं बुद्धिसंयोगं लभते पौर्वदेहिकम् ।
यतते च ततो भूयः संसिद्धौ कुरुनन्दन ॥ ४३ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
tatra taṁ buddhi-saṁyogaṁ labhate paurva-dehikam |
yatate ca tato bhūyaḥ saṁsiddhau kuru-nandana || 43 ||

anvaya.meaning: union of intellect / revival of consciousness

translations.verbatim_quote: On taking such a birth, he revives the divine consciousness of his previous life, and he again tries to make further progress in order to achieve complete success, O son of Kuru.
On taking such a birth, he revives the divine consciousness of his previous life, and he again tries to make further progress in order to achieve complete success, O son of Kuru.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 887 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: King Bharata, who took his third birth in the family of a good brāhmaṇa, is an example of good birth for the revival of previous transcendental consciousness. King Bharata was the emperor of the world, and since his time this planet has been known among the demigods as Bhārata-varṣa.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.43 Tatra, there, in the family of yogis; labhate, tam buddhisamyogam, he becomes endowed with that wisdom; paurva-dehikam, acquired in the previous body. And yatate, he strives; bhuyah, more intensely; tatah, than before, more intensely than that tendency acquired in the previous birth;
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-43-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads buddhi-saṁyogam as 'becomes endowed with that wisdom' — the wisdom-disposition acquired in the prior birth re-attaches to the practitioner in the new family of yogis. The yogi then strives more intensely than the prior tendency, with the prior practice as ground.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.43 – 6.44 There, in that existence, he regains the mental disposition for Yoga that he had in the previous birth. Like one awakened from sleep, he strives again from where he had left before attaining complete success. He strives so as not to be defeated by impediments. This person who h
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-43-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja covers 6.43-44 together: the practitioner regains the mental disposition for Yoga from the previous birth — like one awakened from sleep — and resumes from where he left off, striving so as not to be defeated by impediments. The accumulated substrate carries forward across the gap.

positions.rendering: revives the divine consciousness of his previous life
becomes endowed with that wisdom acquired in the previous body
regains the mental disposition for Yoga that he had in the previous birth

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All three render the compound buddhi-saṁyogam as a re-attachment of the prior practitioner's intellect/disposition to the new birth. Prabhupada flattens to 'divine consciousness'; Shankara holds 'wisdom' (the discriminating buddhi); Ramanuja reads 'mental disposition for Yoga' specifically. The traditional consensus: the prior practice is not lost; the substrate re-attaches and the striving resumes from where it left off.

literal_meaning: There (in such a birth) he obtains that union of intellect from the prior body, and from there he strives further for perfection, O son of Kuru.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 6.43 names the first half of the consolation Krishna offers Arjuna across 6.40-45: the yogi who fell from the practice and was reborn in a favourable family does not start from zero. The buddhi-saṁyoga — the union of intellect — from the prior body re-attaches. Shankara: 'he becomes endowed with that wisdom acquired in the previous body.' Ramanuja: 'he regains the mental disposition for Yoga that he had in the previous birth.' Prabhupada: 'he revives the divine consciousness of his previous life.' From that ground, he strives further (yatate ca tato bhūyaḥ) toward complete perfection (saṁsiddhau). The metaphysics is multi-life; the structural claim is that prior practice persists as accumulated substrate across the gap. The verse is commonly cited as the chapter's core consolation alongside 6.40 and 6.44.
